content.text:
*"I planted the seed, Apollos watered it, but God has 
been making it grow."*{1 Corinthians 3:6}{The binomial coefficients are the most important numbers in all of combinatorics.}{Isaac Newton}

The binomial theorem connects algebra and combinatorics, revealing deep 
structure in expressions like $(x + y)^n$.

content.title: The Binomial Theorem
content.content:
For any real numbers $x$ and $y$ and non-negative integer $n$:
\[ (x + y)^n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} x^{n-k} y^k \]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
When we expand $(x + y)^n = (x + y)(x + y) \cdots (x + y)$, each term in 
the product arises by choosing $x$ or $y$ from each of the $n$ factors.

A term $x^{n-k} y^k$ arises when we choose $y$ from exactly $k$ factors 
(and $x$ from the other $n-k$). There are $\binom{n}{k}$ ways to choose 
which $k$ factors contribute $y$.

content.title: Finding a specific coefficient
content.problem: What is the coefficient of $x^5 y^8$ in $(x + y)^{13}$?

content.title: Binomial Corollaries
content.content:
Setting specific values of $x$ and $y$:

1. $x = y = 1$: $\sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} = 2^n$

2. $x = 1, y = -1$: $\sum_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^k \binom{n}{k} = 0$

3. $x = 1, y = 2$: $\sum_{k=0}^{n} 2^k \binom{n}{k} = 3^n$
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
For $|x| < 1$ and any real $\alpha$:
\[ (1 + x)^{\alpha} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \binom{\alpha}{k} x^k \]
where $\binom{\alpha}{k} = \frac{\alpha(\alpha-1)\cdots(\alpha-k+1)}{k!}$.

marginNotes.text:
Still other seed fell on good soil. It came up, 
grew and produced a crop, some multiplying thirty, some sixty, some a 
hundred times.
